transcript: The idea in economics that if you really want to measure how developed a place is, you don't look at its GDP or its skyline or how many billionaires it produces, you look at the lived experiences of the women in that place. So I thought, okay, let's test it. North and South, same country, same constitution, through that one lens, using the best available data. So, imagine you're standing in front of four maternity wards. One in Bihar, one in UP, one in Tamil Nadu, and one in Kerala. On this day, thousand girls are born in each of these wards. They're all citizens of the same country, and yet, the moment they step out of that ward, they are entering very different worlds. The healthcare systems of these states vary wildly. Infant mortality differs, which is to say not all of these girls are going to make it to age one. A year later, 973 remain in Bihar, 962 in UP, 987
